elements.label: para
elements.text: ORIGINAL ARTICLE

elements.label: sec_0
elements.text:
Integrating multi-camera surveillance with transductive
learning for duplicate removal

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Jorge Batista · Tatiane N. Rios · Matheus Guimarães · Jorge Nery · Cassio V. S. Prazeres ·
Rubisley Lemes · Maycon L. Peixoto · Gustavo B. Figueiredo · Frederico A. Durão ·
Eduardo S. Almeida · Ivan C. Machado · Hérsio M. Iwamoto · Ricardo A. Rios

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Received: 11 June 2025 / Accepted: 1 December 2025 / Published online: 17 February 2026

c The Author(s) 2026

elements.label: sec_1
elements.text: Abstract

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Video surveillance has benefited greatly from advances in artificial intelligence, particularly in computer vision,
and the number of monitored environments has consequently increased, creating new challenges in extracting
relevant information. When multiple cameras cover adjacent areas, overlapping fields of view can cause the
same subject to be detected across cameras, introducing duplicate counts. Although the literature offers estab-
lished solutions, many rely on high-quality recordings and complex, computationally intensive methods, limit-
ing their use on resource-constrained devices. In this work, we address these challenges with a solution that
leverages minimal information about target subjects and uses a transductive strategy to detect duplicates based
on interactions within overlapping fields of view. Experiments in real-world settings show that our approach
suppresses duplicates effectively, making it suitable for deployment on resource-limited hardware. We evaluate
state-of-the-art lightweight models with high inference speed, as well as classical re-identification methods,
in scenarios with low-quality video and constrained devices. The results underscore the effectiveness of the
proposed approach and motivate exploration of re-ID with domain adaptation, as well as anchor-free methods
with weak or semi-supervised learning.

elements.label: para
elements.text: Keywords Multi-camera surveillance · Transductive learning · Duplicate removal · Deep neural network

elements.label: sec_1
elements.text: 1 Introduction

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Video surveillance systems consist of cameras specifically configured to monitor activities in both public and
private settings while subjects act naturally [ 1 ] . The adoption of such systems has been widely increasing to sup-
port several monitoring tasks such as security in general [ 2 ] , accident detection [ 3 ] , urban mobility [ 4 ] , personal
protection equipments [ 5 ] , and costumer behaviors [ 6 ] . Due to the massive number of recorded scenes continu-
ously monitored, it is imperative to use computer vision techniques to extract useful information automatically.

elements.label: header
elements.text: 48   Page 2 of 18

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Another relevant challenge is the integration of multiple cameras in the same area, monitoring the same sub-
jects [ 7 ] . In this situation, the recorded scenes may provide different perspectives and possibly duplicated data.
Therefore, a relevant research question is the identification of duplicate targets to avoid redundancies and present
concise information. To overcome this problem, it is possible to use well-known Deep Neural Network (DNN)
models, including face recognition to detect the same person in different cameras as well as person re-identifi-
cation techniques [ 8 – 12 ] . Besides using DNN, authors have also published related works focused on extracting
distinctive invariant features from images to perform reliable matching between different views of objects and
scenes [ 13 – 15 ] . These models have proven highly effective across various contexts; however, their performance
can be significantly influenced by factors such as infrastructure limitations, video quality, subject positioning, and
the distance between subjects and cameras. Furthermore, as emphasized in the literature, key challenges include
handling occlusions, varying viewpoints, and changes in appearance over time–issues highlighted by recent
studies published in top-tier computer vision conferences and journals over the past two years [ 16 – 20 ] . In such
scenarios, beyond the computational cost of image processing, the feature vectors extracted from detected faces
may vary substantially, even when representing the same individual.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
In this work, we decided to tackle these problems from a different perspective, using fewer details about sub-
jects and creating a semi-supervised approach using transductive learning to detect duplicates according to their
interaction in overlapped fields of view captured by cameras. In a nutshell, our approach is based on a primary
stage, which runs a supervised method to detect target subjects in multiple scenes recorded from different cam-
eras. Secondly, subjects are mapped as points distributed on a space to be later analyzed by a regression method,
whose outputs are used as delimiters to the transition regions. By using delimiters, one can identify overlapped
regions and discard multiple detections for the same subjects. There are several situations where this issue occurs,
such as counting cars on streets, people in stadiums and shows, animals in wild areas, and more. In this work,
we have made some assumptions to assess our proposal carefully: (i) cameras are in fixed positions; (ii) no pre-
defined positions are necessary for the cameras; (iii) high-quality videos are not required; (iv) subjects move
freely in the monitored area; and (v) overlapped areas between cameras may exist, potentially showing duplicated
subjects. In this context, we collected and analyzed videos recorded indoors with multiple cameras positioned to
monitor people in overlapping fields of view.

elements.label: para
elements.text: The contributions of this work can be summarized as follows:

elements.label: list
para
elements.text:
The remaining of this manuscript is organized as follows: we first introduce the problem statement; next, in
Sect. 3 , we present our proposed approach; Sect. 4 shows the experimental setup and the obtained results; finally,
in Sect. 5 , we draw conclusions and discuss future work.

elements.label: sec_1
elements.text: 2 Case study analysis: removing duplicate users in indoor and outdoor environments

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Before providing details about our approach, it is important to detail the environments where the experimental
setup was defined. To carefully analyze our results in both qualitative and quantitative ways, we collected record-
ings from indoor and outdoor scenarios. The indoor one was a computer laboratory, a controlled environment
where users (subjects) work and freely move between desks. The space has two blocks (left and right) with
monitoring cameras to cover the entire area. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the camera responsible for monitoring the

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
left block (in red) can capture all users in that region. However, the camera in the right block (in blue) captures
not only users in that region but also those from the left one positioned in the overlapped field of view (FOV), as
a correspondence problem. As a simple task to show the impact of this issue, by automatically counting subjects
using DNN-based models, wrong results are obtained due to the sum of the same users detected in left (correct)
and right (duplicated) blocks.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Figure 2 exemplifies our indoor environment, highlighting important challenges tackled by our proposal.
Firstly, there is a relevant distance between some subjects and the camera, especially those at the end of the
space. Secondly, occlusions may occur when users are partially hidden behind screens or not directly looking at
the cameras. These challenges affect the performance of approaches using face recognition to discard the same
faces in both cameras.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
The second experimental environment focused on removing duplicate users across multiple cameras monitor-
ing passengers at bus stops. This study was conducted in the context of the Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) system in
Salvador, Brazil. The BRT system serves approximately 30,000 passengers daily, and currently operates with
around 40 buses distributed across three lines and 20 stations. The decision-making process for scheduling bus
departures relies on counting the number of passengers at each station. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , this counting is

elements.text:
influenced by data from multiple cameras. Following the validation of our proposal in an indoor environment, we
deployed it in a more complex setting to support the BRI system's operations.

elements.label: sec_1
elements.text: 3 Proposed approach

elements.label: para
elements.text:
This section describes our proposed approach, which consists of two main stages, as detailed in Algorithm 1 .
In the first stage, we train a supervised model to detect the target subjects, i.e., users who move freely through
indoor and outdoor environments, specifically a computer laboratory and bus stations. The second stage focuses
on automatically generating delimiters that highlight transition regions where user duplication is likely to occur.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Along with our contribution, we also present in this section a strategy using a DNN model trained to crop
regions in images using predefined bounding boxes previously defined by specialists, thus explicitly removing
overlapped fields of view. Our approach does not directly use this strategy; it was implemented as a baseline in
our comparison.

elements.label: sec_2
elements.text: 3.1 First stage—face detection

elements.label: para
elements.text:
The detection used in this stage depends on the problem under investigation (e.g., objects, cars, or animals) and
can be modified according to the readers' needs. Once the focus of our investigation is on users, we could con-
sider both person or face detection. We opted for face detection in this approach due to the presence of numerous
objects in the environment that often occlude people, but leaving mostly their faces visible. All faces in the col-
lected videos were manually annotated, resulting in 2,414 bounding boxes—approximately 600 faces per video.
Next, we employed YOLO (You Only Look Once) [ 21 ] version 11, from the Ultralytics library, in our experi-
ments. Table 1 summarizes the key architectural parameters of this model.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
YOLO is a family of real-time object detection models known for treating detection as a single regression
problem, directly predicting bounding boxes and class probabilities from full images in one evaluation. This uni-
fied and efficient approach makes YOLO particularly suitable for tasks requiring high-speed inference. As shown
in Fig. 4 , YOLO models are generally organized into three major components: backbone, neck, and head.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
The backbone, highlighted in blue in Fig. 4 (Backbone), refers to the portion of the network responsible for
extracting general visual features from the input image. These features encode information about edges, textures,
shapes, and more complex patterns as the network deepens. In YOLOv11, the backbone employs a module
known as C3k2, which builds upon the Cross Stage Partial (CSP) design and uses small convolutional kernels to
reduce computational load while preserving expressive power.

elements.label: cap
elements.text:
Table 1 Summary of key
objectives and research goals
in the YOLOv-H model

elements.text:

elements.label: header
elements.text: Page 5 of 18    48

elements.label: cap
elements.text: Fig. 4 YOLOv11 architecture

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_005_figure_003.png)

elements.label: para
elements.text:
The neck, shown in green in Fig. 4 (Neck), is responsible for refining and aggregating the features extracted by
the backbone to enhance the model's ability to detect objects at different scales. It typically aggregates informa-
tion from different layers or scales to improve the model's capacity to detect objects of varying sizes and contexts.
YOLOv11 uses the Spatial Pyramid Pooling – Fast (SPPF) module in this stage. The SPPF pools features across
multiple receptive fields, enhancing the network's ability to localize small or partially occluded objects.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Finally, the head, illustrated by the red box in Fig. 4 (Neck), is the task-specific part of the architecture that
generates the final predictions. In object detection, this includes estimating bounding box coordinates, objectness
scores, and class probabilities. YOLOv11's head incorporates a Convolutional block with Parallel Spatial Atten-
tion (C2PSA), which improves the model's focus on the most relevant spatial areas in the image, thus refining
detection performance across different tasks, including object detection, segmentation, and pose estimation. The
full structure of YOLOv11, including the backbone, the neck, and the head components, is illustrated in Fig. 4 .

elements.label: para
elements.text:
As discussed later, YOLOv11 is available in several variants (yolo11n, yolo11s, yolo11m, yolo11l, and yolo11x)
each offering different trade-offs between performance and computational cost. According to benchmark reports
from Ultralytics, the yolo11m variant achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 51.5 % on the COCO dataset
while utilizing approximately 22 % fewer parameters than YOLOv8m. This reduction in model size, coupled
with competitive performance, positions YOLOv11 as a promising candidate for deployment in both resource-
constrained and high-performance environments.

elements.label: sec_2
elements.text: 3.2 Second stage—semi-supervised field of view projection estimation

elements.label: para
elements.text:
As previously discussed, the proposed approach begins by aligning frames from different cameras and detecting
bounding boxes around each identified face to serve as coordinate references (Algorithm 1 — Lines 1 to 5). Next,
the coordinates are modeled by regressors to create delimiters for the transition regions between fields of view
(FOV) monitored by a set of surveillance cameras $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}$ . Hence, considering a scenario with two
cameras as stated in our case study, FOV for a camera $s_1$ is projected to recordings from $s_2$ , and vice versa. In
summary, each delimiter works as a separator to avoid repeatedly considering the same subject in different cam-
eras, i.e., the same target subject can be detected in multiple cameras; however, it is analyzed only once.

elements.label: foot
elements.text: Neural Computing and Applications (2026) 38:48

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_005_figure_010.png)

elements.label: header
elements.text: 48   Page 6 of 18

elements.label: para
elements.text:
In practice, the subject detection in overlapping regions can present some imprecision caused by the devia-
tions in the FOV lines or the parallax effect when the subject appears to have a different position relative to the
background between the views of each camera. To attenuate this problem, in our case study, a good estima-
tor for the number of users (U) can be obtained by $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i - E[O]$ , in which $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_i\}$ and
$O = \{o_1, o_2, \dots, o_n\}$ are the total number of detected faces and detected faces inside the overlapping region for
all cameras in S. If the analysis is computed between pairs of cameras and the presence of users inside their FOV
has the same probability, the number of users is estimated by the difference between the sum of the difference
detected and the expected users in FOV.

elements.label: sec_3
elements.text: 3.2.1 Initial setup and finding matches

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Considering our case study, the red edges in the left and right blocks, illustrated in Fig. 2 , define how the cam-
eras must be integrated into our proposed approach. To estimate the FOV delimiter on Camera $s_1$ , we monitor
Camera $s_2$ , looking for detections of people entering the frame of Camera $s_3$ through one of its red edges (Algo-
rithm 1—Line 6). When this happens, the person detected entering the frame of camera $s_2$ should also be present
in the frame of camera $s_1$ , approximately along the FOV delimiter line of Camera $s_1$ that we want to estimate.
Therefore, we want to record the coordinates of this person on Camera $s_1$ to use this information in the estimation
process (Algorithm 1—Lines 7 to 11).

elements.label: para
elements.text:
In the best-case scenario, when a person is detected entering the frame of Camera $s_2$ through one of its red
edges, there will only be one person present in Camera $s_1$ , allowing us to confidently identify them as the same
individual (Fig. 5 a). However, in typical conditions, there may be multiple people present in Camera $s_1$ (Fig. 5 b).
Since no re-identification technique is employed, it is impossible to determine which individual corresponds
to the person detected in Camera $s_2$ , leading to ambiguity. Thus, when a person enters the frame of Camera $s_2$
through one of its red edges, we record the coordinates of each person detected in Camera $s_1$ as a possible match,

elements.label: cap
elements.text:

for matching when a detec-
tion occurs on an selected
edge in $s_2$

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_006_figure_007.png)

elements.label: cap
elements.text:
Fig. 6 Movement status of tracked instances. Moving
instances in green, stationary instances in red

elements.label: cap

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_007_figure_005.png)

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
along with an attribute $p = \frac{1}{J}$ for each set of coordinates, with $J$ being a given number of detections present in $s_{12}$
to represent the probability of each one of these detections being a true match.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
As a person crossing a red edge to appear in the image of Camera $s_2$ is in motion, the candidate detections
for matching in $s_1$ must also be moving. Through tracking, we can identify the detected instances in $s_1$ that are
nearly stationary and eliminate them as potential matches, excluding them from the probability calculations for
the remaining detections. An example of this situation is shown in Fig. 6 .

elements.label: para
elements.text:
To estimate the FOV delimiter on Camera $s_2$ , we follow the same procedure, recording possible matches in $s_2$
for any person entering the frame of $s_1$ through one of its red edges (Algorithm 1—Lines 13 to 18). After process-
ing the frames of both cameras, we end up with a collection of possible matches for each camera, as illustrated
by the blue-scaled dots in Fig. 7 .

elements.label: sec_3
elements.text: 3.2.2 Weighted RAMSAC regression

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Lastly, we execute the regression method called Weighted Random Sample Consensus (WRANSC) [ 22 ] on a
set of possible matches collected from the previous step (Algorithm 1—Lines 21 and 22). This regression is a
robust technique used to model datasets with a significant number of outliers. To best fit inliers, WRANSC runs
an interactive process that randomly samples a subset of data to train a classification model. Next, the classifier
predicts the remaining data as inliers and outliers. The process is interactively repeated, keeping the final model
with the greatest number of inliers. An important contribution of our work is to set weights to all data with the

elements.label: foot
elements.text: Neural Computing and Applications (2026) 38:48

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_007_figure_012.png)

elements.label: header
elements.text: 48    Page 8 of 18

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
probabilities obtained for each possible match. In Fig. 7 , we illustrate the execution of wRANSAC on a set with
possible matches collected from two cameras in our case study.

elements.label: code
sec_2
elements.text: 3.3 Supervised baseline: area detection

elements.label: para
elements.text:
According to the literature, in addition to face recognition, some authors address this problem using overlapping
area detection. In our experiments, the proposed approach was evaluated by integrating the recorded areas from
multiple cameras using Deep Neural Networks (DNNs). Similar to the face detection stage, we created bounding
boxes to highlight regions of interest (ROIs) in all images from the right camera block, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . We
then trained a DNN to identify similar ROIs in new video frames and to discard any faces detected outside these
regions–assumed to be duplicates originating from the left camera block.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
The main advantage of this approach is its simplicity, which makes it easily implemented in different contexts
and infrastructures. On the other hand, its main disadvantages are the requirement for a specific ROI defined in
advance for all application and the need to redefine and retrain the ROI when the camera position changes.

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_008_figure_007.png)

elements.label: sec_1
elements.text: 4 Results

elements.label: para
elements.text:
The analyses performed to assess the proposed following conventional practices in the field of Computer Vision 
(CV) for object detection tasks, which is based on the Intersect over Union (IoU) ratio, $IoU = \frac{GrP}{IoU}$ . It essentially 
computes the performance by comparing the overlapped areas between an expected (ground truth) bounding box 
(G) and the predicted one (P).

elements.label: para
elements.text:
During inference, the confidence $C$ of a bounding box is defined by $C = Pr(Class_i|object) \times Pr(object_i)$ .
By choosing a threshold $\tau_1 \in [0, 1]$ for $C \geq \tau_1$ , we decide whether we should consider the predicted bounding
box as a detected object from Class $i, i=1...N$ . By choosing a threshold $\tau_2 \in [0, 1]$ for IoU $\geq \tau_2$ , we can deter-
mine whether there is sufficient overlap between a predicted bounding box and a ground truth box of the same
class to consider the detection a True Positive (TP). If a predicted bounding box does not sufficiently overlap any
ground truth box (i.e., IoU $< \tau_2$ ), it is counted as a False Positive (FP). Conversely, if a ground truth object is not
matched by any prediction with IoU $\geq \tau_2$ , it is considered a False Negative (FN).

elements.label: para
elements.text:
After calculating the number of TP, FN, and FP, we can derive important measures such as Precision, Recall,
and F1-Score. In summary, Precision assesses the exactitude of the model by computing the rate of correctly pre-
dicted detections over the number of predicted detections, while Recall evaluates the completeness of the model
by computing the rate of correctly predicted detections over the number of elements expected to be detected.
F1-Score represents a harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. The Average Precision (AP) is a metric that com-
bines Precision and Recall values over a range of $\tau_1$ , given a fixed $\tau_2$ . Two other metrics, based on AP, that are
widely used to compare performance between models are mAP@50 and mAP@50-95. The mAP@50 (mean
Average Precision at IoU = 0.50) considers a detection correct if the IoU is at least 0.50, providing a smoother
evaluation. In contrast, mAP@50-95 is a more comprehensive metric that averages AP across multiple IoU
thresholds, from 0.50 to 0.95 in steps of 0.05, offering a more stringent and balanced assessment of detection
accuracy.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
We started our experiments by evaluating different pre-trained YOLOv11 architectures (yolo11n, yolo11s,
yolo11m, yolo11l, and yolo11x) on a Raspberry Pi 4, a limited device usually considered in Edge Computing
applications. The first analysis was focused on assessing the overall performance of such models in detecting
labeled faces in our dataset. According to the curve F1-score $\times$ confidence (Fig. 9a ), all models presented great
performance varying from 0.87 to 0.91, showing no significant difference between them. A similar behavior was
noticed in the Precision-Recall curve shown in Fig. 9b .

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Next, we analyzed such models in terms of mean inference time, memory usage, and CPU consumption.
According to the results presented in Table 2 , yolo11n demonstrated the fastest mean inference time (0.507 s)
with a memory footprint of 345 MB and a CPU usage of 319 % . Given our objective of running our proposal on
resource-constrained hardware, we selected yolo11n to proceed with our experiments.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
In the next phase of our investigation, we fine-tuned the yolo11n model using images from our dataset. To
ensure robust and generalizable results, we adopted a 4-fold cross-validation strategy, where each fold comprised

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
videos from a distinct location. This setup allowed for a balanced trade-off between having a sufficient number of
images for both training and testing while maintaining diversity across folds.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Each fold was trained with a batch size equal to 16, an optimizer based on a stochastic gradient descent method
(AdamW algorithm), and a learning rate equal to 0.01 for 100 epochs. After completing the training, we selected
the value of $\tau_1$ that showed the highest F1-Score for each fold. For F1-Score, Precision, and Recall, a $\tau_2=0.7$
was empirically considered to maximize the results. As shown in Table 4 , the final model exhibited a substantial
improvement in detection performance following fine-tuning with our dataset. Notably, yolo11n was approxi-
mately 30 times faster than yolo11x (16.212s vs. 0.507s, as shown in Table 2 ) and achieved a higher F1-score
after fine-tuning (Table 3 ) than all versions without fine-tuning (Fig. 8 ).

elements.label: para
elements.text:
While the previous results demonstrate the feasibility of deploying YOLOv11n on resource-constrained
devices, we also replicated the experiments using NanoDet [ 23 ] , a fully convolutional, one-stage, anchor-free
object detector. NanoDet's main advantage is its lightweight design and high inference speed, which make it
well suited for embedded and mobile scenarios. Table 4 reports NanoDet's performance across the same cross-
validation folds used for YOLOv11n, enabling a direct comparison under identical conditions.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
In our experimental setup, YOLOv1n achieved considerably better results. Given its favorable CPU utiliza-
tion, memory footprint, and inference time on the Raspberry Pi 4, we selected YOLOv1n for the subsequent
evaluation stages. Nonetheless, based on our findings, NanoDet remains a highly suitable alternative, especially
for deployments on more constrained devices.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
The experiments designed to evaluate our contribution followed a four-step methodology: (i) users were manu-
ally labeled in both camera views to establish the ground truth; (ii) a face recognition model was assessed to

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
determine its ability to identify the same individual across different cameras; (iii) a baseline model based on area
detection, commonly used in the literature on correspondence problems, was executed; and (iv) our proposed
approach was applied to automatically integrate scenes and eliminate duplicate user representations.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Figure 10 illustrates a teaching scenario in which two cameras monitor the same space with a partially over-
lapping field of view. Our objective is to evaluate whether students captured by the left camera, Fig. 10a , can be
accurately recognized by the right camera, Fig. 10b .

elements.label: para
elements.text:
The experiment began by detecting all faces in both camera views. Subsequently, each detected face was con-
verted into an embedding vector using the VGG-Face model as a feature extractor. In the next step, embedding
vectors from the left camera were compared to those from the right camera using the Euclidean distance metric, as
commonly adopted in the literature. The VGG-Face model considered in our experiments was accessed through
the DeepFace library [ 24 ] . Figure 11 presents the distances between pairs of faces. Due to space constraints, only
the 10 closest matches are highlighted. Given the distance between the subjects and the cameras, none of the faces
captured by the left camera were correctly matched with those from the right camera. This outcome is expected,
as the same faces are captured from different angles by the two cameras, which significantly affects the similar-
ity between their corresponding embeddings. This experiment underscores the limitations of using classical face
recognition as a re-identification (re-ID) method in settings with low-quality cameras or when subjects are far
from the sensors. Even in the absence of occlusions, small face sizes, pose and illumination changes, and motion
blur reduce the discriminative content of the extracted embeddings, leading to low match rates and failure to cor-
rectly resolve multiple entries.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Given the insufficient results previously presented, we investigated the feasibility of removing duplicates by
employing an area detection model for people counting. Figure 12 shows the actual and estimated numbers of
users, represented by the yellow and blue lines, respectively. As one may notice, this approach effectively pro-
duces results that align closely with the expected behavior. It is important to note that this model detects regions
of interest that are manually defined during the training phase. Its main limitation, however, is the need to manu-
ally annotate the overlapping regions whenever the monitored scenario changes, which in turn requires retraining
the detection model.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
In the second experiment, we assessed the performance of our approach by automatically detecting transition
regions between fields of view (FOV). The obtained results are presented in Fig. 13 . By visually analyzing these
plots, it is possible to notice that our approach outperforms the area detection in Fold 1 and presents similar
results to the remaining ones.

elements.label: foot
elements.text: Neural Computing and Applications (2026) 38:48

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_011_figure_012.png)

elements.label: header
elements.text: 48   Page 12 of 18

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_012_figure_003.png)

elements.label: para
elements.text:
An additional evaluation was conducted using the estimated FOV lines to perform the counting on the manu-
larly annotated users instead of the detections generated by the face detection model used in our proposal. In sum-
mary, we want to determine whether errors in counting users were caused by the automatic detection of lines or
by user detection errors. Results presented in Fig. 14 emphasize that some errors are indeed caused by the model
used to detect users, suggesting that our approach may provide superior results with an improved version of the
user detection model.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Finally, in addition to visually analyzing the results, we employed the following regression validation metrics
to evaluate our findings: Mean Absolute Error, Mean Squared Error, Root Mean Squared Error, and Mean Abso-
lute Percentage Error. The obtained results are shown in Table 5 . The significant outcomes of our approach are
illustrated by its predictions, which closely align with the manual annotations of regions of interest and field of
view (FOV) lines. Thus, our proposal was successfully deployed into a resource-constrained device, typically
used in Edge Computing applications, providing significant results in detecting duplicated subjects in multi-
camera monitoring without requiring high-quality recordings or user identification.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
After fine-tuning the best model in the indoor environment, we evaluated the performance of our proposal in
real-world scenarios supporting the BRT system. In Fig. 3 , we illustrate this application, including the FOV line
(right-most image) generated by our approach. Consistent with the previous results, Fig. 15a and b highlight the
performance of our method in a given station within a specific time interval. The strong performance of our solu-
tion underscores its significance, while the mean discrepancies in the real count (Fig. 15a ) are attributed to image
quality issues, as the system may not detect passengers located far from the camera. In summary, our approach

elements.label: header

elements.text:

elements.label: fig

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
provided MAE = 1.220, MAPE = 0.255, MSE = 2.820, and RMSE = 1.679, and the annotations' metrics were
MAE = 0.060, MAPE = 0.005, MSE = 0.100, and RMSE = 0.316. These results confirm the effectiveness and
high quality of our proposed method.

elements.label: sec_1
elements.text: 5 Final remarks

elements.label: para
elements.text:
In this work, we address the challenging task of reducing the impact of duplicated information in multi-camera
surveillance systems. This problem arises when the system monitors the same space, creating overlap in regions
covered by two or more cameras. In the literature, there are well-known approaches to overcome this situation,
such as subjects' re-identification to detect duplicates. However, the performance of such models can be signifi-
cantly affected by factors like the analyzed target, video quality, and the distance between subjects and cameras.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Our contribution introduces an efficient and adaptable approach for automatically detecting overlapping fields
of view (FOV) without relying on high-quality recordings or subject re-identification. By combining classifica-
tion and regression methods, our method significantly reduces the need for detailed subject features, making it
suitable for both indoor and outdoor environments and especially valuable in resource-constrained devices, such
as those used in edge computing. The results demonstrate that our method achieves performance levels compara-
ble to manually labeled models, while maintaining flexibility: if monitoring cameras are repositioned, the system
automatically recalculates the FOV lines, ensuring adaptability to dynamic environments.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
As highlighted in our experiments, yolo11n was approximately 30 times faster than yolo11x and achieved a
higher F1-score after fine-tuning with our approach than all versions of YOLOv11 without fine-tuning, reinfore-
ing the efficiency and accuracy of our detection pipeline. Besides, an additional evaluation was conducted by

elements.label: foot
elements.text: Neural Computing and Applications (2026) 38:48

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_013_figure_010.png)

elements.label: header
elements.text: 48   Page 14 of 18

elements.label: cap
elements.text:
Fig. 13 Comparison between
the real person count and
counting misusers by
different FOV, and the
detections from our model.

elements.label: fig
elements.text: ![Figure](figures/s00521-025-11716-2_page_014_figure_003.png)

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
using the estimated FOV lines to count manually annotated users instead of relying on the outputs of the face
detection model. As shown in our results, this analysis revealed that some counting errors were due to inaccu-
racies in user detection rather than in FOV estimation. This suggests that our approach could yield even more
accurate results when integrated with a more precise user detection model, further demonstrating the robustness
and potential of our proposed solution.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
As future work, we plan to evaluate our approach using alternative regression methods. Although wrANSCAN
offers a simple and effective solution, it operates as a linear model to detect overlapping regions. However,
depending on the spatial distribution of subjects, a non-linear mapping may yield better results, particularly as
the number of monitored cameras increases. Another promising avenue for future work is the adoption of weak
supervision or semi-supervised learning, which can markedly reduce labeling effort, especially in non-stationary
settings where class patterns evolve over time. To this end, a lightweight face detector can propose candidate
boxes, high-confidence detections can be retained with temporal smoothing, and object trackers can propagate
boxes across frames. An active-learning loop can include a human in the loop to resolve uncertain cases. For FOV
boundaries, candidate lines can be estimated from motion-density heatmaps and optical-flow discontinuities, fol-
lowed by targeted spot checks where uncertainty remains high. This strategy can substantially reduce annotation
costs while preserving accuracy.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Our pipeline currently assumes that a subject crossing the edge will reappear in the adjacent camera, an
assumption that may not hold under severe occlusion or complex multi-camera geometry. As future work, this
handoff can be generalized from a deterministic rule to a probabilistic mechanism that fuses scene topology and
tracklet evidence, improving robustness across layouts. This can be achieved by adding short-horizon trajectory
extrapolation to bridge missed detections during occlusions. As discussed by Lin et al. [ 25 ] , single-frame location
anticipation has shown that subsequent locations can be predicted from a static keyframe by exploiting smooth

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
motion. Adapting this idea allows us to keep tracklets alive through occlusions and postpone duplicate suppres-
sion until the predicted reappearance window expires, which directly targets the observed failure mode.

elements.label: para
elements.text:
Finally, while we explored a classical face-based re-identification approach, the lower performance highlights
the need to extend our pipeline with domain adaptation and anchor-free detection. For re-ID, promising directions
in the field of computer vision continue to be identified.

elements.text: Neural Computing and Applications (2026) 38:48

elements.label: half_para
elements.text:
include lightweight backbones with unsupervised adaptation to new camera domains, leveraging pseudo-labels
and temporal consistency to reduce supervision while respecting resource constraints. For detection and track-
ing, adopted anchor-free heads that directly predict object centers and sizes can simplify hyperparameter tuning
and reduce latency on edge devices. Together, these extensions aim to improve robustness across layouts and
illumination conditions, including viewpoint and scale changes, while preserving the efficiency that motivates
our deployment.
